text: When you are late for a meeting with me, I get pretty frustrated. I wish that you would be on time more often.
label: assertive
source: SAMHSA Anger Management Manual - Session 1, p.11
notes: Assertive way of handling a friend being repeatedly late

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Sorry I'm late again.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: When you are late for a meeting with me, I get pretty frustrated. I wish that you would be on time more often.

context_basis: SAMHSA Session 1 p.11 describes scenario: 'if you were upset because a friend was repeatedly late for meetings'

text: Joe, I've noticed you've been late for the last few days when I've come to pick you up. Today, I realized that I was starting to feel frustrated and a bit taken for granted. When you are late, we are both late for the meeting, which makes me uncomfortable. I like to be on time. I'm wondering if you can make an effort to be on time in the future.
label: assertive
source: SAMHSA Anger Management Manual - Sessions 7&8, p.42
notes: Frank's assertive dialogue with Joe using Conflict Resolution Model

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Sure. Is something wrong?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Joe, I've noticed you've been late for the last few days when I've come to pick you up. Today, I realized that I was starting to feel frustrated and a bit taken for granted. When you are late, we are both late for the meeting, which makes me uncomfortable. I like to be on time. I'm wondering if you can make an effort to be on time in the future.

text: Damn, I forgot them again.
label: passive-aggressive
source: Paterson, R. J. (2022). The Assertiveness Workbook (2nd ed.). New Harbinger Publications., Ch.2, Alan's story
notes: PA excuse for not completing agreed tasks

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Did you bring the questionnaires?

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: Damn, I forgot them again.

context_basis: Paterson Ch.2 Alan's story: Alan repeatedly 'forgot' to bring agreed-upon questionnaires to therapy sessions
exclusion_reason: PA label requires knowing Alan's weeks-long pattern of strategic forgetting; single instance indistinguishable from genuine mistake.

text: No, I really wanted to be on time, but the bus was late.
label: passive-aggressive
source: Paterson, R. J. (2022). The Assertiveness Workbook (2nd ed.). New Harbinger Publications., Ch.2
notes: PA denial of intent with plausible excuse

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: You're late again.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: No, I really wanted to be on time, but the bus was late.

context_basis: Paterson Ch.2 describes this as PA denial of intent when confronted about chronic lateness
exclusion_reason: PA label requires knowing speaker's chronic pattern of rotating plausible excuses; single excuse indistinguishable from honest explanation.

text: Honey, alcoholism is a disease, so I can't be held responsible for any of my actions when I'm drunk.
label: passive-aggressive
source: Paterson, R. J. (2022). The Assertiveness Workbook (2nd ed.). New Harbinger Publications., Ch.16
notes: PA avoidance of responsibility through self-labeling

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Honey, alcoholism is a disease, so I can't be held responsible for any of my actions when I'm drunk.

exclusion_reason: PA label requires knowing speaker strategically uses disease framing to dodge accountability; text alone reads as aggressive blame-deflection.

text: You're right, I should never have asked you to work late. I'm the worst manager in the company!
label: passive-aggressive
source: Paterson, R. J. (2022). The Assertiveness Workbook (2nd ed.). New Harbinger Publications., Ch.16
notes: PA admitting guilt to avoid responsibility for change

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: I didn't appreciate being asked to work late.

conversation.speaker: B
conversation.text: You're right, I should never have asked you to work late. I'm the worst manager in the company!

context_basis: Paterson Ch.16 presents this as exaggerated self-deprecation to avoid genuine responsibility for change

text: I notice that the lawn hasn't been cut in some time. I remember that a couple of months ago we agreed that it would be your job to cut it once a week. I don't like to see it get that long, but I feel frustrated nagging you about it all the time. I'd like you to do it once a week, as we agreed earlier. That way I won't feel I have to get after you about it.
label: assertive
source: Paterson, R. J. (2022). The Assertiveness Workbook (2nd ed.). New Harbinger Publications., Ch.16, DESO script example
notes: Assertive DESO script - Describe, Express, Specify, Outcome

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: I notice that the lawn hasn't been cut in some time. I remember that a couple of months ago we agreed that it would be your job to cut it once a week. I don't like to see it get that long, but I feel frustrated nagging you about it all the time. I'd like you to do it once a week, as we agreed earlier. That way I won't feel I have to get after you about it.

text: Jonas, I'm concerned that you're not returning my calls because I have offended you in some way. Could that be true?
label: assertive
source: Paterson, R. J. (2022). The Assertiveness Workbook (2nd ed.). New Harbinger Publications., Ch.16
notes: Assertive inquiry about symbolic value rather than confronting with assumptions

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: Jonas, I'm concerned that you're not returning my calls because I have offended you in some way. Could that be true?

text: Ivan, when I arrived at the river, I realized that you hadn't sent the paddles on ahead. I was frustrated because that meant we didn't have enough for everyone to practice with. I'd like you to come up with a plan to ensure that on future trips everything gets packed and sent, and then run it past me. That way, I can feel more confident taking people out there and I won't be hassling you afterward.
label: assertive
source: Paterson, R. J. (2022). The Assertiveness Workbook (2nd ed.). New Harbinger Publications., Ch.15, DESO example
notes: Assertive full DESO script - Describe, Express, Specify, Outcome

I'd like you to come up with a plan to ensure that on future trips everything gets packed and sent, and then run it past me. That way, I can feel more confident taking people out there and I won't be hassling you afterward.

conversation.text: I won't allow you to take advantage of me and I won't attack you for being who you are

text: If I keep telling the kids to do their chores they will get annoyed at me and might not like me or think I am a good mum
label: passive
source: CCI Assert Yourself - Module 10
notes: Passive thinking preventing assertive action [Label 'passive' inferred; CCI Module 10 terms this 'unassertive thinking' without explicit passive/aggressive categorization.]

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: If I keep telling the kids to do their chores they will get annoyed at me and might not like me or think I am a good mum

text: All kids get annoyed at their mum sometimes, it doesn't mean they don't like them. It is important for the kids to learn how to do chores. To be a good mum sometimes I will have to get the kids to do things they don't like. They may thank me for that later.
label: assertive
source: CCI Assert Yourself - Module 10

text: Some tasks could safely be ignored. Others could be done in such a way that he wouldn't be asked to do them again.

notes: PA strategic thinking about work avoidance

conversation.text: Some tasks could safely be ignored. Others could be done in such a way that he wouldn't be asked to do them again.

text: You're entitled to get your own way, even after making commitments to others. You're not responsible for your actions.
label: passive-aggressive
source: Paterson, R. J. (2022). The Assertiveness Workbook (2nd ed.). New Harbinger Publications., Ch.2, Checkpoint table

conversation.speaker: A
conversation.text: You're entitled to get your own way, even after making commitments to others. You're not responsible for your actions.

exclusion_reason: PA label comes from Paterson's checkpoint table describing PA belief system; generic 'you' is descriptive, not accusatory; text reads as aggressive direct attack.
